text: for blowjob. But then I see her face has changed in the split-second I looked away. Della appears absolutely and completely drained of color. The hint of humor on her face is replaced by an expression of desperation. I know what she’s going to do before she does. At least, I think I so. Della’s right hand covers her stomach. she hunches over and throws up beside Lori Wilson’s new backpack with Post Malone’s face on it. This is the start of it. # Della is… gifted . She excels in volleyball and almost every single class in school. But excel isn’t quite right. To excel in something, you’d have to try . Della doesn’t. It’s effortless to her. She is naturally good at everything. One time at the Twin Oaks ping pong table, I watched her beat a guy, six years older, in a best out of five match. She found out later the guy had been state champion for two years in a row, and before that, Della had played exactly two games of ping pong in her entire life, one of which had been with me about a half hour earlier. I’m not jealous. I’m really not. Sure, there are times I wish I was right up there with her, but that’s okay. Della doesn’t take it for granted. She knows it. There’s no way not to know, really, but she doesn’t flaunt it. The thing about Della is she’s genuinely nice without being sickeningly so. One time, in the second grade, I threw up. It was on the school playground, and I’d been playing on the monkey bars way too long. The end of recess bell went off and I walked about fifteen feet before I turned and hurled my cafeteria pizza in a row of marigolds. That was over eight years ago and people have never forgotten. When Della throws up, there’s a beat of absolute silence, and Lori seems to realize how close Post Malone’s face has come to getting covered with vomit. She wrinkles up her nose and uses her sneakered foot to slide her bag to the front of her desk. But then, I watch her slowly realize it was Della who threw up, and this pure and completely sincere sympathy washes over Lori’s face. She stands up and puts her hand on Della’s shoulder, and over the next few moments, ends up getting told by Mrs. Eckenrode to personally escort Della to the School Nurse. This is unusual and makes me feel uneasy. Nothing bad really ever happens to Della. Like I said, she’s gifted. Della’s good luck is really annoying to me, and a little tougher to accept, only if because I seem to have a black cloud over me at times. If there’s something to stub my toe on, I’ll find it. I am a splinter magnet. I will spill any liquid in a glass as efficiently as oil company tankers, washing over homework or art projects, important papers, you name it. The day Della throws up in class, it’s like the well of good luck she’s been storing has suddenly run dry. Somehow, karma has been keeping track of the absence of bad things in her life, until it makes up for it, all at once. # I see Mom at the kitchen counter, wiping something down with a sponge. She glances at me over her shoulder, her cleaning the important thing here. “Della’s resting so don’t play your music.” I use one foot and then the other to take my shoes off on the little rug in the front hall. I lean down and put my sneakers next to Della’s. “Did she throw up again?” Mom doesn’t bother looking, intent on the circular motions of cleaning the counter. “Once, right after she got home.” I don’t say anything in response, and walk the narrow staircase upstairs to our shared bedroom. My parents had us when they were young. She was nineteen, and he was twenty. Mom quit college one week into her first semester, and Dad worked night shift while he was going to college during the day. She worked as a waitress at a diner right up until her water broke during a lunch shift. I’m sure it was tough. It’s crazy to think of being twenty years old, a new parent, and have college and a job and responsibilities. I get it. But their failure to use birth control isn’t our fault. So anyway, we share a bedroom. Dad has been talking about moving to a bigger house for as long as I can remember. But his words and any actual action have been at war for years. Almost every time the news is on, he’ll mumble something along the lines of “Economy’s in the dumps. Rich are gettin’ richer every damned day, and the workin’ man ends up screwed.” Truth is, Della and I don’t mind sharing a room. We never have. I figure it has something to do with being in a womb together for nine months. If we can get along in a tiny house like that, then sharing a bedroom is a cake walk. Della is lying on her bed when I creep into the room. She’s clutching her pillow, and her legs are curled up. I stare at her for a moment, wondering if that’s how she slept inside Mom. Even though I haven’t made a sound, her eyes flutter open and she turns her head slightly to look at me. Della offers me a crooked smile that fades as quickly as it arrived, a moment of sunshine poking through a cloudy sky. “How ya feelin’?” I step closer, see the damp strands of hair pasted to her pale, sweat-beaded forehead. On the nightstand between our beds, there’s an unfolded washcloth sitting on a plastic Zip-Lock baggie. I haven’t even set my backpack down, but I use the damp washcloth to gently wipe Della’s forehead, smoothing the hair away from
